meta.purpose: Define terminology boundaries, common misunderstandings, and self-checks for issue decomposition judgment.
meta.load_condition: Always loaded when this domain is active.

standard_terms.definition: A thin end-to-end implementation that delivers one observable behavior from user input to system output.
The first vertical slice that proves the architecture works end-to-end at production quality.
Human-In-The-Loop: an issue or step that requires human interaction to complete (decisions, approvals, manual verification).
Away-From-Keyboard: an issue or step that can be completed autonomously by the agent without human interaction.
Observable, testable conditions that define when an issue is complete. Without them, an issue is a wish.
The process of determining whether an issue is ready for execution, needs information, requires human decision, or should not be done.
The explicit line between what an issue includes and excludes, preventing scope creep within the issue.

banned_terms.why: 'Small' is not a decomposition criterion. A small horizontal task is still a fragment. Size is irrelevant; deliverability is what matters.
banned_terms.replace_with: an independently deliverable issue with clear acceptance criteria

banned_terms.term: we'll figure out the scope as we go
banned_terms.why: Scope discovery during implementation is scope creep. Define the boundary before starting.
banned_terms.replace_with: let me define the acceptance criteria and scope boundary first

banned_terms.why: Describing an issue by layer (frontend, backend, database) is horizontal slicing. Describe it by behavior instead.

banned_terms.term: I'll just start on this and see what needs to be done
banned_terms.why: Starting without triage risks working on invalid, incomplete, or misaligned issues.
banned_terms.replace_with: let me triage this issue first to confirm it's ready for execution

misunderstandings.wrong: Breaking work into small pieces is the same as vertical slicing.
misunderstandings.correct: Small pieces can still be horizontal. Vertical slicing means each piece delivers end-to-end behavior. Small horizontal pieces deliver structural progress without observable value.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Small ≠ Vertical. A small task can be a fragment. A vertical slice is defined by the behavior it delivers, not its size.
misunderstandings.why: Confusing small with vertical leads to many small fragments that provide no value until assembled — the same problem as large horizontal slices.

misunderstandings.wrong: The tracer bullet should be a throwaway prototype.
misunderstandings.correct: The tracer bullet is production-quality code that establishes the architecture. It is not a prototype to be thrown away — it is the first vertical slice that subsequent slices extend.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Prototype vs. Tracer bullet. A prototype is disposable exploration. A tracer bullet is the first production slice that proves the architecture.
misunderstandings.why: Making the tracer bullet disposable means the architecture isn't proven under production conditions. The team builds on an unvalidated foundation.

misunderstandings.wrong: All dependencies should be resolved before starting any issue.
misunderstandings.correct: Only blocking dependencies must be resolved. Non-blocking dependencies can be worked around with stubs or interfaces. Waiting for all dependencies creates bottlenecks.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Blocking dependency vs. Non-blocking dependency. Blocking = cannot start without it. Non-blocking = can start but need it for full integration.
misunderstandings.why: Treating all dependencies as blocking serializes work that could be parallelized, slowing the project without improving quality.

misunderstandings.wrong: Triage is just prioritization — deciding what to do first.
misunderstandings.correct: Triage determines whether to do something at all. Prioritization determines the order. Triage answers 'should we do this?' not 'when should we do this?'
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Triage ≠ Prioritization. Triage is a quality gate (is this issue valid and ready?). Prioritization is a scheduling decision (what order?).
misunderstandings.why: Conflating triage with prioritization means invalid issues get prioritized instead of rejected, wasting effort on work that shouldn't be done.

self_check: Does each issue deliver observable value on its own, without requiring other issues to be complete?
Is every issue a vertical slice (end-to-end behavior), not a horizontal layer?
Does every issue have acceptance criteria that define what 'done' looks like?
Are dependencies between issues explicitly stated?
Has each issue been triaged before being picked up for execution?
Is there a tracer bullet — a first slice that proves the architecture works?
